exemplar.title: Building and Managing and Online Business

detailedExemplarData.title: Building and Managing and Online Business

strengths: The student identifies a range of ATL skillsâincluding communication, research, thinking, and self-managementârelevant to both the learning goal and product development.
weaknesses: Descriptions of communication skills lack data on outreach outcomes (e.g., number of contacts made, responses received).
Research skills are mentioned without showing how specific findings informed product decisions.
The tutorial screenshot is presented without explanation of how its content resolved a particular challenge.
Thinking skills are described superficially without a concrete example of gap analysis leading to design choices.
Self-management is referenced abstractly, with no details of tools or scheduling systems used.
There is no direct evidence of how ATL skills solved specific problems in product completion (e.g., technical issues during site deployment).
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student demonstrates awareness of the ATL framework and has noted multiple skills applied during the project. However, to meet higher criteria, each skill must be linked to explicit examples and outcomes. For instance, communication should be evidenced by quantified engagement metrics; research findings should be tied to design or strategic decisions; and self-management should reference specific planning tools such as a Gantt chart. This level of detail will illustrate not only which skills were used but also their tangible impact on the projectâs success.

strengths: The student has developed comprehensive research questions to guide the investigation.
A student-designed criteria table is used to organize the areas to be evaluated.
An action plan with tasks and completion dates demonstrates initial project structuring.
weaknesses: Success criteria remain largely qualitative, lacking measurable benchmarks and deadlines.
Research questions are too broad and not directly aligned with the defined success criteria.
The learning goal is stated in overly general terms without specific, measurable outcomes.
Product goals lack concrete deliverables and quantifiable targets.
The criteria table lacks clear performance standards and threshold values for each element.
The action plan omits resource allocation details and sequencing of dependent tasks.
The connection between the learning goal and personal interest is described broadly without specific evidence or past experience.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: While the student has clearly invested effort in outlining research questions, designing evaluation areas, and drafting a task-based action plan, the planning stage needs stronger emphasis on specificity and measurability. Success criteria should include quantifiable metrics (for example, website traffic targets or client satisfaction scores) and firm deadlines. Research questions must be tightly focused to directly serve those criteria, and the learning and product goals need to be reframed with clear, concrete outcomes. Finally, resource assignments and task dependencies should be mapped out to ensure feasibility and coherence of the plan.

strengths: Collecting and presenting client satisfaction survey data shows a solid attempt to gather external feedback and validate service quality.
weaknesses: The self-assessment rubric stops short of assigning performance levels and lacks justification for each criterion rating.
Survey results are reported without critical analysis of potential bias or implications for improvement.
There is no evaluation of the final product against the predefined success criteria (usability, cost, customer feedback).
The website URL is listed without usability testing results or analytics to measure performance against goals.
Claims of improved organizational skills are unquantified; no data (e.g., percentage of tasks completed on time) support the reflection.
Logo feedback (100% positive) is not analyzed in relation to the aesthetic criteria defined earlier.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The reflection section reflects a commendable effort to incorporate client feedback via surveys, but it lacks depth in critical analysis and evidence-based evaluation. To strengthen the reflection, the student should assign justified levels to each rubric criterion, interrogate the survey data for biases and learning points, and systematically compare product outcomes to the original success metrics. Quantitative evidence of skill growth (such as a timetable adherence rate) and specific usability or analytics results will provide a richer, more credible assessment of both product and personal development.

annotations.comment: Your one-point rubric summarizes strengths and development areas but stops short of scoring performance levels. Self-assess each criterion with a justified level.
Survey data shows unanimous praise but lacks critical analysis. Reflect on potential biases (e.g. sample size) and outline next steps for improvement.
Reflection outlines growth but doesnât evaluate the final product against each success criterion. Add evidence for how the website, cost, and customer feedback met your defined targets.
Listing your site URL is helpful, but you havenât evaluated its usability or performance against your success criteria. Include user test results or analytics.
Success criteria remain qualitative. Add quantifiable metrics (e.g. website traffic, engagement rates, client satisfaction survey scores) and deadlines.
Your research questions are comprehensive but too broad. Narrow them to align directly with your success criteria and desired deliverables.
You mention using communication skills to reach out but donât detail outcomes. Cite numbers of contacts made, responses received, and how this influenced your strategy.
Collecting client satisfaction survey data demonstrates a strong evaluation approach. You effectively gathered external feedback to validate your service quality.
Research skills are listed but lack linkage to product development. Show how specific findings (e.g. competitor pricing models) shaped your service offerings.
Your learning goal âto understandâ is too broad. Frame it with specific, measurable outcomes (e.g. âidentify and document five key operational steps for launching an online business by Mayâ).
The product goal lacks concrete deliverables. Specify measurable targets (e.g. âacquire at least one paying client and deliver three branded marketing assetsâ).
Student-designed criteria table defines areas but lacks clear performance standards and thresholds for each criterion; include measurable benchmarks for aesthetics, cost, and customer satisfaction.
Action plan lists tasks and completion dates but omits resource allocation and sequential dependencies. Map out who, what, and in which order tasks occur for feasibility.
You include a tutorial screenshot but donât explain how its content resolved a specific challenge. Link video insights to an action you took and its result.
Thinking skills description is superficial. Include a specific example of how gap identification led to targeted research that informed your business model.
Self-management is referenced abstractly. Provide details on your scheduling tools or tracking systemâe.g. a Gantt chartâto demonstrate effective time management.
Applying ATL skills to product completion needs concrete examples. Describe how critical thinking and time management directly solved a named problem (e.g., site deployment glitch).
You note improved organizational skills but lack data (e.g. percent of tasks completed on time). Quantify your learning impact to strengthen your reflection.
100% positive logo feedback is encouraging, but reflect on how the logo meets your aesthetic criteria (e.g. color variety, brand alignment) for deeper insight.
Your connection between learning goal and personal interest is expressed broadly. Strengthen this by citing specific past experiences or skills gap that motivated your learning objective.
